data.title: Turbocharging Energy Stocks With The ERX ETF
data.description:
In an August 7, 2025, Seeking Alpha article on the Direxion Daily Energy 2X Shares (ERX), I concluded with the following:

Steady oil prices in August 2025, and the U.S. administration’s support for fossil fuels through its “drill-baby-drill” and “frack-baby-frack” policies, support U.S. oil and oil-related companies’ earnings. Trading these volatile companies using ERX and ERY with time and price stops could generate significant results.

NYMEX crude oil futures were trading below $65 per barrel, the XLE ETF was at $42.20, and the ERX ETF was at $50.67 on August 7. The events in late February and March caused all three to rally and remain higher than the August 7 price on April 20.

Energy was the best-performing sector in Q1 2026

The Middle East conflict lifted traditional energy prices over the first three months of 2026. The energy composite posted a 55.71% gain from the end of 2025 through the end of March 2026. The individual Q1 2026 gains were as follows:

  • NYMEX WTI and ICE Brent crude oil futures rallied 76.56% and 70.73%, respectively.
  • NYMEX RBOB gasoline futures rose by 86.82%. Gasoline refining spreads moved 127.91% higher.
  • NYMEX heating oil futures, which are a proxy for other distillate oil products, posted a 93.91% gain. Distillate refining spreads gained 123.5%.
  • Chicago ethanol swaps rose 27.99%.
  • Coal for delivery in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, rose 26.1%.
  • Coal for delivery in Newcastle, Australia, rose 32.5%.

The only energy commodity to decline in Q1 2026 was U.S. natural gas, which fell 21.76% as the market moved from peak demand to the shoulder season of low demand. However, European natural gas futures moved higher, with U.K. natural gas prices posting a 75.55% gain, and Dutch natural gas prices moving 80.24% higher.

The XLE rose over 37% in Q1 but has pulled back in early Q2

The fund profile for the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) states:

XLE’s top holdings include:

Rising energy prices boosted the shares of energy-related companies and the XLE ETF in Q1.

The monthly chart shows that the XLE rose 37% in Q1 2026, moving from $44.71 to $61.26 per share. The XLE fell below $56 per share on April 20, 2026, as energy prices declined amid prospects for an end to hostilities in the Middle East.

U.S. oil companies could remain big winners after the war with Iran

The reasons why U.S. energy companies and the XLE should continue to profit after the war with Iran ends are as follows:

  • The United States is the leading oil and gas-producing country.
  • The U.S. administration supports increasing oil and gas production to achieve energy independence and lead the world in exports.
  • Sanctions on Russia and supply concerns over Middle Eastern oil and gas make U.S. energy more attractive.

U.S. oil and gas producers and related companies in oil services and refining should continue to profit over the coming months and years.

ERX turbocharges the XLE

The fund summary for the Direxion Daily Energy Bull 2X ETF (ERX) states:

ERX’s top holdings include:

ERX’s holdings include many of the energy companies held by the XLE ETF. In Q1 2026, the XLE rose by 37%.

The chart shows that the leveraged ERX ETF rose 84.4%, moving from $55.85 to $103.01 per share. The gain for ERX more than doubled the gain of XLE for the first quarter of 2026.

Both the XLE and ERX corrected since the end of Q1. At around $55.50 per share on April 20, the XLE was 9.4% lower, while at around $84.20, the leveraged ERX ETF fell by 18.3%.

At $84.20 per share, ERX had $244.58 million in assets under management. ERX trades an average of 479,972 shares per day and charges a 0.91% management fee. The $1.49 dividend yields 1.77%.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF grades for ERX are the highest possible A+ in the momentum category due to its price action over the past few months. While ERX earns a B+ in the liquidity category, dividends receive a D+, and the 0.91% management fee receives a D- in the expense category. ERX’s leverage and extreme volatility in energy commodities and related stocks earn a failing grade in the risk category.

The Direxion Daily Energy Bear 2X ETF (ERY) is ERX’s converse product that rises when traditional energy-related stocks decline.

At $12.46 per share, ERY has moved higher since the end of Q1 when ERX posted a 18.3% decline.

The daily chart shows the 21.2% gain from $10.28 on March 31, 2026, to $12.46 per share on April 21, 2026.

The potential for high rewards comes with high risks

Leverage comes at a steep price: time decay. If energy-related stocks decline or remain stable, ERX will lose value. If energy-related stocks rally or remain stable, ERY will lose value.

ERX, ERY, and other leveraged ETFs are tools suitable only for short-term risk positions on the long or short side of energy-related stocks. When approaching energy share exposure with ERX or ERY, time and price stops are appropriate, as the gearing can lead to oversized losses. Moreover, stable energy share prices will likely cause ERX and ERY to decline, as time decay erodes their value. Swaps and options that create leverage dramatically increase risk. The SEC website highlights the significant risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as ERX and ERX.

Leverage requires planning. Any risk position in ERX or ERY requires stops and profit horizons that reflect the ETF’s potential for extreme volatility. While it is appropriate to increase profit horizons when a leveraged ETF moves in the anticipated direction, adjusting stop levels will protect profits and capital. When ERX or ERY move contrary to expectations, sticking to stop levels will protect capital and limit losses.

I am rating ERX a hold in the current environment, as both ERX and ERY are trading tools and not investment tools. I favor ERX, as there is a compelling case for continued profits in energy-related stocks.

data.title: BITO: Distributions Run Dry
data.description:
I last wrote about the ProShares Bitcoin ETF (BITO) in January and warned that it is not an income fund. Of course, with a headline 77% yield, it was widely regarded as one, but that was not the fund's intended strategy, and there was a risk of distributions drying up as Bitcoin (IBIT) dropped. Since that article, the last three monthly dividends have taken a huge dive. The April payout was only $0.0061, which is less than 1% of the December payout of $0.7432.

This article looks at what is behind the massive cut and what could happen over the rest of 2026.

BITO Strategy

With income ETFs all the rage in recent years and 100% yields not uncommon, BITO may be mistakenly grouped alongside these ETFs. Despite the significant drop in recent payments, its headline TTM yield is still 73%. However, BITO is different — it does not use an options overlay to boost yield, and its distributions derive from profits in bitcoin derivatives such as futures and swaps.

It was never intended to be an income fund.

Due to its holdings, BITO also has a large holding in money market funds, which are used as collateral. This pays interest and likely accounts for the recent small distributions.

The Variable Distribution

BITO primarily gains exposure to bitcoin through futures contracts. As per the prospectus:

Bitcoin Futures Contracts – Standardized, cash-settled bitcoin futures contracts traded on commodity exchanges registered with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (“CFTC”). The Fund seeks to invest in cash-settled, front-month bitcoin futures.

Here are the current holdings, which show April futures.

The futures positions are rolled each month, and the assumption is that any profits are distributed. On the other hand, if there is a loss, no distributions would be made.

However, it is not quite as simple as that, and there is no strong correlation between a monthly gain/loss and the next distribution.

This is illustrated by the two charts below, which show bitcoin fell in five months in a row from October 2025, but distributions remained large and only fell in February.

Also note that the two red months in early 2025 did not lead to a missed dividend. The distribution did fall by about 50%, but payments continued. How was BITO able to do that?

Distribution Size is a Decision Made by the Fund

There are two points to make about the imperfect relation between bitcoin's monthly gains and subsequent distributions.

Firstly, futures contracts need to be rolled before the settlement dates. For CME bitcoin futures, that is the last Friday of the month. So the monthly bitcoin gain/loss is not an exact reflection of the gain/loss on any particular monthly contract (but it's usually pretty close).

Another reason for the discrepancies comes from factors like contango or backwardation when the contract is rolled. As per the prospectus:

...when a bitcoin futures contract is nearing expiration, the Fund will “roll” the futures contract, which means it will generally sell such contract and use the proceeds to buy a bitcoin futures contract with a later expiration date. When rolling futures contracts that are in contango, the Fund would sell a lower priced, expiring contract and purchase a higher priced, longer-dated contract. The price difference between the expiring contract and longer-dated contract associated with rolling bitcoin futures is typically substantially higher than the price difference associated with rolling other futures contracts.

However, these factors account for only minor differences between bitcoin price movements and subsequent distributions. The main reason is that profits/losses are not recognized each roll but at the end of the year. As per the ProShares site:

Each of the Funds gets exposure to oil or crypto futures contracts, or swap on a commodity or crypto index or an index of futures contracts, as applicable, by investing in a wholly owned subsidiary which, in turn, invests in such futures contracts or swap agreements. For tax purposes, each Fund recognizes any net income of its subsidiary, including gains on such futures contracts or swap agreements in which its subsidiary invests, as an ordinary dividend on the final day of the subsidiary’s tax year.

The final day of the tax year in 2026 will be September 30th. Up until that time, the fund has a lot of flexibility to adjust distributions depending on what it thinks the annual profit/loss will be at the end of the year.

The amount of each Fund’s monthly dividend distribution (if any) is intended to estimate the Fund’s current required calendar year distribution allocated equally over the remaining months of the calendar year.

It is important to remember that the dividend distribution amount for each Fund may change significantly from month to month. In some months, a Fund may not make any dividend distributions at all. For example, a Fund may not make distributions if its subsidiary has had significant recent losses or if the distributions previously paid by the Fund have already covered the Fund’s expected required calendar-year dividend distribution.

That flexibility means the fund may overestimate (overpay) dividends through some periods. It can be seen by the NAV erosion through 2024 and 2025 even when Bitcoin rallied and total returns were positive. The fund paid out more than it made.

Just to clarify, the NAV and total return were almost identical in 2022 because BITO did not pay any dividend. That's also the case in 2026 since the distributions have been tiny.

Implications for the Distribution

The current tax year started on October 1st, 2025, which means there has been a large loss in the period so far.

As already shown, the fund made payments in October through January and only cut in February. That resulted in NAV erosion.

While bitcoin posted a small gain in March and is doing well in April, that won't necessarily mean the fund managers will be confident enough to raise the distributions again. Remember, BITO is actively managed, so the payments depend on their decisions and outlook. I think they may be cautious over the coming months, as raising it too soon risks more NAV losses. That said, keeping it at these levels could lead to more losses in AUM, so they will have some difficult decisions to make.

Of course, a rally in Bitcoin would make their decision much easier.

Bitcoin Outlook

Bitcoin has been making higher lows since February and held up well during the stock (SPY) drop throughout March. You could say it acted somewhat as a safe haven, and there may also be an element of it hedging against inflation as investors fear a second wave of inflation brought on by elevated oil prices. Most of all, though, I think bitcoin was oversold. The final capitulation came when gold and silver crashed - not because of anything related to Bitcoin, and they all found support around the same time in early February.

When we look at the Bitcoin chart, the recovery does not yet look convincing in the context of the preceding drop, and there is an active head and shoulders pattern with a target way down at $34 thousand. The pattern looks similar to the last major top in 2021.

What worries me most is the relative weakness compared to other assets such as stocks. The S&P 500 has just ripped to new all-time highs, showing risk appetite is strong, but Bitcoin is still languishing. While it could recover to test the head and shoulders neckline around $85 thousand, I'd need to see higher highs above $98 thousand as well as higher lows before I would be confident of a new uptrend.

Assuming Bitcoin reaches $85k and either turns lower or just moves sideways, BITO's NAV for the 2025-2026 period would still be negative. Distributions are therefore not guaranteed, and while I would expect management to deliver some payments, they may stay small and below the $0.74 paid out in December.

Short-term, taking advantage of a rally to $85k would be better with a low-cost ETF like the iShares Bitcoin ETF (IBIT). It outperforms BITO in the majority of periods and timeframes due to BITO's complex structure and expenses.

Bitcoin futures are in contango, as the May contract is trading around $200 higher than the April contract, so the roll decay will drag on NAV. This contango is likely to continue as long as Bitcoin stays steady, and backwardation is only usual during steep sell-offs.

Conclusions

The distribution cuts this year underline the point I made in my last article: BITO is not an income fund. The monthly distributions are variable and are decided by management based on annual distribution levels for tax reasons; they do not necessarily reflect the bitcoin price movements in the previous months.

After three tiny payments this year, and after some recovery in the bitcoin price, management may decide to make some higher distributions in the months to come. However, I suspect they will remain at low levels until Bitcoin rallies significantly, as they risk eroding NAV even further.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: What The Selloff Gets Wrong
data.description:
AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (ASTS) is down about 14% in pre-market trading on Monday, a rough start to the week, and building on the 6% plunge last Friday.

The stock recovered slightly at the market open and was down around 9% at open.

The stock has been on my radar for a while, but it’s just now entering attractive territory, and understanding why it's selling off matters before you buy into the stock. So, before I get into why the stock has gone out of the market’s favor, let’s take a look at the business itself.

The business:

In the simplest terms, ASTS is a company that puts cell towers in space, not satellites that require a special dish on your roof. Basically, your phone connects directly to their BlueBird satellites orbiting 700 km (or lower) above Earth, the same way it connects to a cell tower on the side of a highway, with no new hardware, SIM card, etc.

Since every other satellite connectivity solution before this required you to buy something, like a Starlink dish, ASTS collapses that friction to zero, which is why the addressable market is everyone who has ever driven through a dead zone and is not bound to one specific niche of early adopters.

ASTS doesn't sell directly to consumers; instead, they sell wholesale capacity to the carriers, who wrap it into their existing plans. AT&T, Verizon, Vodafone, and over 50 other mobile network operators globally are already signed up, collectively representing nearly 3 billion subscribers. ASTS provides the sky, and the carriers handle everything else, including billing, customer service, distribution, and brand. The carrier takes roughly half the revenue, ASTS keeps the other half, and has almost no customer acquisition cost.

The latest generation of satellites, known as Block 2 BlueBird, is the largest commercial communications array ever deployed in low Earth orbit, measuring roughly 2,400 square feet of antenna surface each, larger than the first-generation Block 1 satellites, which measure almost 700 square feet. Size matters here because it's what makes connecting to an unmodified phone physically possible, as the satellite antenna does the heavy lifting, so your phone doesn't have to.

Now, the company is currently working toward a specific operational threshold, according to the company’s CFO, Andrew Johnson, on the Q4 '25 earnings call back in March

For 2026, AST SpaceMobile’s global workforce is intensely focused on completing our Block 2 BlueBird satellites to support the orbital launch of 45 to 60 total satellites during the year, as we work towards commercial service activation in the second half.

Whether the company hits that target is being questioned by the market and even priced in pre-emptively.

The competition:

2025 was a good year for ASTS, as the company finally started being revenue-generating but still non-profitable. Total operating expenses for FY2025 were $260 million against $70.9 million in revenue, meaning they’re burning roughly $3.50 in operating expenses for every $1 of revenue earned. That ratio only resolves one way, through commercial service activation at scale in the second half of the year. If that slips, the burn rate becomes a bigger point of concern.

It was 2025 that investors really started looking at ASTS, along with other aerospace plays, as the stock was up around 250% for the year, from trading at $23 to $83 in early January of this year.

It all started when the stock went on an absolute tear in mid-2024, running from the low $20s all the way to nearly $130 at its peak, which attracted retail investors who dreamed about owning the next SpaceX (SPACE) before it was obvious. SpaceX's Starlink is the elephant in the room, and I discuss the latter in more detail here. Every time ASTS needs to put something in orbit, they write a check to Blue Origin, SpaceX, or others, but every time SpaceX needs another satellite up, the marginal cost is nearly zero.

Obviously, there’s a counterargument. Starlink's direct-to-cell has been slow, low-bandwidth, and largely limited to basic messaging, while ASTS is targeting full voice and broadband on unmodified handsets.

According to the company’s CEO, Abel Avellan,

2026 is the year we scale commercial operations. We are the only company capable of delivering 4G and 5G and, in the future, 6G broadband speed sufficient for voice calls, voice over LTE, live video calls, streaming, and full Internet access directly to modified devices. Our technology is anchored by our ability to manufacture the largest commercial communications array ever placed into low Earth orbits. Creating a durable technology advantage. Our satellites enable digital beam forming and are capable of multi-carrier aggregation in multiple frequencies, supporting simultaneous users per beam, behaving like a terrestrial cell tower from space.

Technically, at these levels, the market is asking: Is ASTS a generational infrastructure play that gets acquired or scales into a $100B business? Or is it a well-funded startup that SpaceX eventually takes out of the race? The stock at $130 was priced in the former, but the stock at $75 this morning is somewhere in between. The way I’m looking at it, that gap is where the opportunity lives. I think the sell-off is overdone.

The company has had 5 first-generation Block 1 satellites in orbit since September 2024, plus 1 Block 2 next-generation satellite launched in December 2025, but it's the Block 2s that actually move the needle, carrying roughly 10 times the capacity of their predecessors. While what happened yesterday with BlueBird 7 changes some math, it doesn’t invalidate the equation, so let's get right into it.

Why the dip:

BlueBird 7 was supposed to be the second Block 2 in orbit. Instead, Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket, which is the launch vehicle ASTS hired to carry it up, had a problem with its upper stage. The latter didn't fire correctly, and BlueBird 7 got dropped off too low. The satellite's own thrusters don't have enough power to climb to the correct altitude, so it's being de-orbited and will burn up on re-entry. While the insurance covers the hardware (around 2% to 20%), the $3.9 billion in liquidity should absorb the schedule hit and the cost.

While one lost satellite is not an existential event for a company capitalized the way ASTS is, here's what it does do: it puts pressure on a timeline that had no slack in it to begin with. Getting from 6 operational satellites today to 45 by December 31st was already an ambitious ask at one to two launches per month.

The market is repricing that execution risk right now, and that’s where the initial 15% dip comes from. With the stock essentially a leveraged bet on a launch schedule, priced at 140x EV/Sales, in a week when that launch schedule just took its first visible hit, the pullback is understandable.

Valuation:

Valuation is not attractive per se, but definitely more attractive than it was at all-time highs in January. At $75, the stock is currently trading at a forward price/sales ratio of 138x, above the sector median of 1.1x, and a forward price/book ratio of 13.3x, again above the sector median at 1.87x.

But traditional valuation multiples don't apply for a company at this stage and for a stock that moved on sentiment and sympathy with the sector at large. You don't value ASTS on what it earns today but on whether the execution and guidance both hold into the next two years.

The company is guiding for 2026 to be an

“an inflection point as we enter commercial service with our initial MNO partners, while also continuing to generate revenue from the commercial gateway and government strategies. Before the impact of commercial service revenue later in the year, we expect revenue to at least double versus 2025….Looking ahead to 2027, with a large-scale constellation in orbit, we see a really, really strong outlook for both commercial and government service revenue. 2027 will be the first full-year impact of commercial service revenue as the AST SpaceMobile cellular broadband service becomes available in some of the best markets worldwide to hundreds of millions of subscribers via a low-friction service offering provided when the subscriber needs it most.”

The company is also expecting government revenue to strengthen into 2027, depending on:

“contract outcomes…We see the opportunity in 2027 approaching $1 billion in annual revenue, importantly comprised of revenue both long-term contracted or highly recurring in nature, subject to achievement of commercial and government service objectives.”

The company is working towards commercial service activation in the second half of the year, and since that guidance was set before BlueBird 7 was lost, there is now a real question of whether the timeline holds.

The satellite design is not the problem; what failed was New Glenn's upper stage, and this wasn't even an isolated anomaly. This marks the second New Glenn upper stage issue in the same month, following a separate ground anomaly at Blue Origin's cleaning and testing facility in Florida. I’m looking at the dip as an attractive entry point, mainly because the selloff is being driven by sentiment and schedule anxiety, not by any fundamental deterioration in the business itself.

The next logical move for management is to lean harder on Falcon 9. The company reiterated its guidance for:

“approximately 45 satellites in orbit by the end of 2026…BlueBird 7 would have been AST SpaceMobile’s eighth deployed into low Earth orbit and is one of many planned for its space-based cellular broadband network. The company is currently in production through BlueBird 32, with BlueBird 8 to 10 expected to be ready to ship in approximately 30 days.”

This is the first launch failure, so the market is pricing in exactly that, along with some panic. I think the risk-reward is favorable at current levels.

data.title: Hitachi: Recent Developments Support A 'Buy'
data.description:
I'm keeping Hitachi, Ltd. (HTHIY) (HTHIF) as a "Buy"-rated name. The latest news flow confirms that its financial prospects remain promising.

My prior April 3, 2025, article touched on how HTHIY will thrive on sustainability- and technology-related advancements.

Nuclear Deals Are A Catalyst

The firm aims to expand the top line for "Hitachi Energy Segment/HES" by a mid-teens CAGR between FY24 (YE Mar 2025) and FY30 to $33 billion. Analysts' projection of HTHIY's group-level annualized increase during the same timeframe is a more modest +8% (source: S&P Capital IQ).

The above-mentioned numbers send me a clear message about HES's critical role in boosting the company's future turnover.

Last month, SA News highlighted a "nuclear power project in Tennessee, where GE Vernova (GEV) and Hitachi will build BWRX-300 small modular reactors (SMRs)."

HES is currently too reliant on the Power Grids sub-segment, which contributes over nine-tenths of its operating profit. Looking ahead, it could move towards a more balanced business mix with this massive U.S. nuclear venture.

As per the recent media report, the Tennessee asset's CAPEX alone is estimated at $40 billion, or more than HES's targeted decade-end revenues.

Lifecycles Of Various Power Categories

Also, I like the fact that HTHIY has chosen SMRs over "Large Nuclear Reactors/LNRs." The former's shorter buildout period as outlined above should imply faster payback.

GEV also revealed "a memorandum of understanding with Hitachi to explore opportunities to deploy the BWRX-300 small modular reactor in Southeast Asia/ASEAN" in March.

My take is that HES's focus on this geographical region is wise.

ASEAN represents "a quarter of growth in global energy demand by 2035" based on a March 26, 2026, "Associated Press" piece. This write-up also indicated that 5 of the countries in this bloc have announced atomic-linked roadmaps.

In my opinion, the future performance of HTHIY's Energy business could surpass expectations thanks to nuclear-related tailwinds.

New Rail Win Signals Profitability Pivot

In early March, HTHIY disclosed that its subsidiary "Hitachi Rail will design and deliver the rolling stock and signaling systems" for "Turin Metro Line 2/TML2."

I think this represents a major shift from its Mobility segment's legacy asset-heavy model.

The TML2 transaction involved a bundled sale of HTHIY's higher-margin traffic management software and the comparatively lower-return passenger vehicles.

It has also cross-sold its profitable data analytics service branded as "HMAX", on top of the typical hardware sales. At its October 2025 AI strategy briefing (S&P Capital IQ transcript), management unveiled the long-term target of raising HMAX's penetration by 10x from two thousand trains currently.

Hitachi Rail has the bargaining power to charge a premium for HMAX, in my view. The latter has been able to cut clients' upkeep-related expenses by around 15 percent according to actual case studies.

The Mobility division recorded an EBITA-to-revenue of 8.9% for the most recent 3QFY2025. That's 390bps below the company-wide figure. I see this as an illustration of this business' profitability enhancement potential with deals like TML2.

Portfolio Reshaping Is In Progress

HTHIY issued a press release in late March regarding a 40-60 joint-venture with Oki Electric (OKIEY) to house their combined "Automated Teller Machines" operations.

This move is aligned with its goal to improve "Return On Invested Capital" or "ROIC" from 10.9% for FY2024 to 12%-13% by FY27.

ATMs are part of HTHIY's "Digital Systems & Services" or "DSS" segment. That was the group's most profitable business in the latest quarter, boasting an EBITA-to-revenue of 15.2%.

DSS's outperformance is mainly attributable to the Systems Integration solutions. But these margins could have been higher without the capital-intensive ATM manufacturing activities.

I anticipate that the operating profitability of DSS will be enhanced following the deconsolidation of the ATM unit. It's reasonable for me to assume that HTHIY will likely restructure other low-ROIC entities going forward.

Also, the funds freed up from such initiatives could help to boost ROICs indirectly by shrinking the capital base. HTHIY has a ¥0.1 trillion buyback authorization in effect from the end of Jan. to the end of Apr. this year. It has already completed more than half of this plan by March 31.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Downside Factors

Many nations might defer their nuclear programs in the event of any unfortunate high-profile accidents that raise safety concerns. That would hurt HES's growth outlook.

The rise in HMAX's take-up rate for the Rail operations could be slower than expected. This can be due to pricing considerations or doubts about its effectiveness.

My Closing Thoughts

I'm still bullish on this stock.

HTHIY is now trading at a forward FY26 EBITDA multiple of 12.5 times, as per S&P Capital IQ.

Siemens (SIEGY) and GEV, its listed Mobility & Energy comparables, are valued by the market at forward EV/EBITDAs of 30x and 45x, respectively.

This name is undervalued based on my peer benchmarking exercise. I expect the future growth, margin, and ROIC improvements to act as re-rating triggers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank of Hawaii Stock Is Worth The Premium Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Bank of Hawaii Corporation (BOH) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 24%. While the company just reported a Q1 earnings miss, investors largely shook that off, with the stock trading close to flat as adjusted earnings were more in line with expectations. The bank is enjoying meaningful tailwinds from fixed reinvestment, and this can power earnings growth for some time. While shares are a bit expensive, this premium is justified.

In the company’s first quarter, Bank of Hawaii earned $1.30, which missed expectations by $0.02 even as revenue grew 13% to $192 million. Results were weighed down by $1.3 million of investment securities losses; otherwise, the quarter was in line with expectations (these losses are generally excluded from analyst consensus). While not the fastest-growing bank, BOH has a very conservative capital structure and long-running margin tailwinds that were both made clear in the quarter.

Looking first at its balance sheet, deposits were $21 billion, down 1% sequentially, given seasonal drawdowns after the typical late Q4 build. Average deposits were up about 1% from last year. During the quarter, its cost of interest-bearing deposits declined by 22 bps, a strong pull-through of Q4 rate cuts, to 1.72%. Over the past year, it has cut deposit rates by about 44 bps. About 27% of BOH’s balances are non-interest-bearing (“NIB”). At $5.65 billion, these were up about 1.5% from last year.

The Hawaiian deposit market is a bit of an oligopoly, dominated by five players with a small presence from the national banks. Because of this, deposit pricing tends to be relatively low, enabling stronger pass-through of Fed rate cuts onto borrowers. Within this market, BOH has the leading market share. Given its already leading position, I expect long-term deposit growth to be fairly muted (closer to 2% than 4%).

On the asset side, it has $14.2 billion of loans, and average loans were flat over the past year. Relative to most regional banks, BOH has more consumer exposure with ~$8 billion of balances, where its commercial lending is just $6.2 billion. Over 80% of the consumer portfolio is tied to the housing market. With almost all of its loan portfolio secured by physical assets, loan losses tend to be low, with recoveries given default fairly high.

About 93% of its loan book is in Hawaii, so local economic factors are extremely important. As you can see below, the Hawaiian economy has meaningfully underperformed the broader U.S. over the past decade, primarily due to a much sharper decline during COVID-19 and slower subsequent recovery. While U.S. GDP returned to pre-COVID levels by early 2021, Hawaii did not do so until mid-2023. The state’s economy has much less exposure to the tech industry, a key driver of U.S. growth, and is much more dependent upon tourism. This recovery was further hampered by a devastating wildfire in 2023 and a weaker Japanese yen, as foreign tourists are a key driver of growth.

Given its reliance on tourism, I view the Hawaiian economy as having lower potential growth than the U.S. as a whole, as that is not an industry (unlike tech or manufacturing) with meaningful long-term productivity growth. That said, the economy is actually performing well compared to its potential, with unemployment of just 2.2%, roughly half the national average. For consumer credit quality, I view the level of employment as being more important than the potential growth rate—so long as people are employed, they tend to be able to make payments.

I remain cautious about the overall pace of travel spending, given broader affordability challenges in the economy. Hotel RevPAR (revenue/available room) fell in 2025 for the first time since COVID-19, and travel spending has become increasingly reliant on activity by luxury travelers in a sign of the “K-shaped” economy. Obviously, the future of the Iran War is unknowable, but I remain concerned that the market is underpricing the potential impact of the oil market disruption. If inflation stays higher for longer, that could further crimp travel spending, which is highly discretionary. As such, I expect little if any economic growth in Hawaii over the next 12 months and for unemployment to potentially tick up slightly towards 2.5%-3.0%.

BOH also has a $7.9 billion investment securities portfolio. This is a portfolio of high-quality fixed income, but like many banks, it was purchased largely when rates were lower. As such, it only yields 2.72%. With each quarter, some of the portfolio matures, and BOH can reinvest proceeds at current market yields. As a result, its portfolio yield is up 29 bps from a year ago. It will take multiple years for the portfolio to fully roll over, but this will eventually add $1.50-$2.00 of EPS, assuming a 3.5-4% long-term yield.

BOH generated $151 million of net interest income (“NII”) in Q1, up 4% sequentially. Given the fact that Q1 has two fewer calendar days, it is unusual for banks to report a sequential increase in NII, but its net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded a strong 13 bps from Q4 to 2.74%, given the significant reduction in deposit costs. This more than offset lower loan yields. NIM was up 42 bps from last year, thanks to fixed reinvestment and lower deposit costs. With ongoing fixed asset reinvestment and 0-1 Fed rate cuts, we should see BOH’s NIM approach 3% over the next year.

During the quarter, Bank of Hawaii set aside $2 million for credit losses. Net charge-offs were just $1 million, reflecting the strength of its underwriting, and BOH modestly added reserves during the quarter. It ended the quarter with $12 million in nonperforming assets, down $2 million sequentially. This was also down by $5 million from a year ago. We typically see an improvement in consumer credit quality during Q1 as tax refunds allow borrowers to catch up on past due statements.

BOH has set aside $147 million for loan losses. This covers ~1.04% of loans and is more than 10x nonperforming assets. That is an extremely strong reserve position, as I like to see coverage of 2.5x. This reserve position provides the company with a significant buffer should economic conditions worsen and delinquencies build. To a large extent, BOH has already reserved for a worse economic environment, and so earnings should be fairly well protected against a modest rise in delinquencies.

Beyond its solid reserves position, BOH maintains an excellent capital posture with a 12.1% tier 1 common equity capital (CET1) ratio. Now, given its size, BOH does not need to include the $247 million unrealized loss in AOCI within its capital calculations. Adjusting for this, BOH would still have an adjusted capital ratio of about 10.4%, which is very healthy, as I view 9.5-10% as the necessary amount of capital to safely run its business. Given its strong capital position, BOH can return capital to shareholders, including $15 million of buybacks during Q1. Shares also offer a 3.5% dividend yield.

Looking forward, I see BOH earning $5.90-$6.20 this year, assuming stable credit provisions. While I see some downside risk in the Hawaiian economy, its excess reserve position more than makes up for this. Beyond that, the bank has another $1.25+ of earnings growth over the next two years from fixed reinvestment, not to mention ~$4 of excess capital per share.

BOH shares are ~13x earnings, above my 9-12x fair value for regional banks. However, with a run-rate EPS above $7.50 over the next 18 months, shares could trade at $90 (12x run-rate earnings). BOH has an elevated multiple today because its securities portfolio provides a clear runway for earnings growth, and BOH can grow into its multiple. With ~12% upside to fair value and a 3.5% dividend, I view BOH as an attractive buy even with shares at a 52-week high.

data.title: Honeywell Q1 Preview: Portfolio Transformation In Focus, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Honeywell (HON) will be releasing its Q1 this Thursday, and ahead of that release I still view shares as a ‘hold.’

I provided commentary on HON in February, and in that release, I opined that shares were fairly valued near its all-time highs. I spoke positively about HON’s portfolio transformation strategy. However, with shares already trading near its forecasted price target, I didn’t see a reason to chase shares any further.

Since my update, the stock has not changed meaningfully. It’s down about 4.5%, but that’s not exactly material enough to justify a change in stance.

An important update that warrants attention is the just announced deal regarding Brady Corporation’s (BRC) acquisition of HON’s solutions and services business. In my view, this will further support HON’s strategy priorities, though I don’t necessarily believe this is an earth-shattering announcement ahead of the Q1 release.

What Is The Outlook For HON Stock?

As I look back at the guidance provided during the Q4 call, I believe that HON laid out a fairly positive path for 2026.

HON is targeting organic sales growth of 3% to 6% and 6% to 9% earnings growth. That outlook is supported by record backlog, continued pricing discipline, and productivity actions across the automation businesses as the company prepares for the Aerospace separation in Q3.

Speaking of Aerospace, the unit is expected to grow at a high single-digit pace, while Building Automation should deliver above mid-single-digit growth. In addition, Process Automation is likely to start slower before improving in the second half as backlog converts.

In my view, all of this paints the picture of a company with steady operational momentum heading into what will likely be a transitional year for HON.

The recently announced agreement for BRC to acquire HON’s Productivity Solutions and Services (“PSS") business fits directly into this narrative. PSS generated roughly +$1.1B in sales in 2025, and I believe its divestiture further simplifies HON’s portfolio and sharpens its focus around higher-margin, less cyclical automation and software-driven businesses.

How Will The Brady Corporation Acquisition Impact Honeywell International?

The just announced sale of HON’s PSS unit to BRC is, in my view, another logical step in HON’s ongoing portfolio simplification. PSS generated approximately +$1.1B in 2025 sales and operates in mobile computers, barcode scanning, and printing solutions.

Yes, these are solid businesses, but ones that sit a bit outside of HON’s growing emphasis on higher-margin automation, software, and process technologies.

At roughly 8x EBITDA, the valuation appears reasonable and clean. In my view, HON is monetizing a non-core asset at an attractive multiple while freeing up management attention and capital ahead of the Aerospace spinoff in Q3.

This follows the same playbook we’ve seen with other recent divestitures. Streamline the portfolio, reduce complexity, and sharpen the company’s identity around Automation.

Financially, I don’t believe the deal will move the needle in a major way in the near term. Strategically, however, I believe it supports the message the management team has been delivering for over a year.

What To Watch When Honeywell Reports Q1?

With HON reporting this Thursday, the first thing I’ll be watching is whether the strong order momentum from Q4, up 23% with a record +$37B backlog, is carrying into Q1.

I believe that backlog is really the backbone of the 2026 outlook, so any commentary on conversion rates and end-market demand will matter.

A second area is Aerospace. Management has already guided to high single-digit growth for the year, but Q1 commentary around commercial OE ramp timing and defense strength will help set expectations for how smooth that trajectory actually is.

Third is Automation, particularly Building Automation and Process Automation. These are increasingly becoming the core identity of Honeywell post-spin, so any color on pricing, software adoption, and margin expansion will be important.

Finally, I expect some discussion around the BRC transaction. While it is not financially transformational, investors will likely want clarity on timing, use of proceeds, and how this further shapes the post-spin Honeywell.

More broadly, any updated detail on the Aerospace spinoff timeline and remaining portfolio actions will likely be closely watched as well. At this point, messaging around simplification matters just as much as the quarterly print itself, in my view.

Is HON Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At the end of the day, I continue to view HON as a 'hold' heading into Q1 earnings.

For me, not much has really changed in a meaningful way since my last update. Shares are still trading near the upper end of their valuation range, not far from all-time highs, and, in my view, already reflect a lot of the positives we’ve seen over the past year.

Order trends remain strong, backlog is at record levels, and the 2026 guide points to steady mid-single-digit growth with continued margin strength.

On top of that, the portfolio transformation continues to progress, with the Aerospace spinoff still on track and additional divestitures like the just announced BRC transaction further simplifying the structure.

But that’s also part of the reason I remain neutral at current levels. In my view, investors are already paying for this cleaner, more focused HON. The strategic direction is clear, but I believe much of that clarity is already reflected in the stock’s performance and valuation.

So while I am positive on the long-term direction of the business, I don’t necessarily see a compelling near-term re-rating opportunity from here. For existing holders, I think it’s a steady name to own. For new investors, however, I would still prefer a better entry point.
